query: pathophysiology of Eisenmenger syndrome including changes in pulmonary vessels and central pulmonary artery

retrieved_docs.title: Atrial Septal Defect -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The smooth muscle layer in the vascular wall thickens as the pulmonary vessels remodel. This increase in muscle mass raises the resistance to flow in the pulmonary circuit. Pulmonary hypertension develops due to the rise in vascular resistance and, subsequently, pulmonary pressures. When pulmonary pressures equal systemic pressures, the shunt across the ASD reverses, allowing deoxygenated blood to flow into the left atrium and, ultimately, the systemic circulation. This reversal of the shunt due to pulmonary hypertension leads to Eisenmenger syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most common defects leading to Eisenmenger syndrome are ASD, VSD, and PDA defects. The following 3 main processes result in the ultimate reversal of a left-to-right into a right-to-left shunt: Vasoconstriction, which is due to imbalances in pulmonary vascular tone, followed by Vascular remodeling, which is due to the proliferation of pulmonary vascular smooth muscle, and finally Thrombosis, which is caused by the increased resistance of blood flow.

retrieved_docs.title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histological examination of the vasculature in Eisenmenger syndrome would resemble other forms of pulmonary hypertension. Increased proliferation of vascular smooth muscle, evidence of lung parenchymal scarring (pulmonary fibrosis), and decreased luminal caliber due to cellular proliferation in the vascular walls all are indicative of a PAH process.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Ductus Arteriosus -- Clinical Significance -- Patent Ductus Arteriosus
retrieved_docs.content: Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) is a condition in which the ductus arteriosus fails to close after birth. A PDA is one of the most common cardiac defects associated with congenital rubella syndrome and may severely impact an infant's quality of life. [9] The PDA leads to the generation of a left-to-right shunt since the blood pressures in the aorta are much higher than in the pulmonary artery. If allowed to remain patent, the pressure inside the pulmonary circuit will rise, leading to pulmonary hypertension. Failure to adequately address this pulmonary hypertension may lead to cyanosis and shunt reversal (Eisenmenger syndrome). Late complications of Eisenmenger syndrome include arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death. The development of this syndrome indicates irreversible changes to the cardiac and pulmonary vasculature, and lung/cardiac transplants are the only viable treatment option. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Eisenmenger syndrome (ES) is a constellation of symptoms that arise from a congenital heart defect and result in large anatomic shunts. Due to anatomic variations present at birth, hemodynamic forces initially result in a left-right shunt, which develops into severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and elevated vascular resistance. Ultimately, due to increased pulmonary vascular resistance, the left-to-right shunt will become a right-to-left shunt, resulting in significant hypoxemia and cyanosis. This activity discusses the evaluation and treatment of Eisenmenger syndrome and further defines the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Eisenmenger syndrome is a constellation of symptoms that arise from a congenital heart defect and result in large anatomic shunts. Due to anatomic variations present at birth, hemodynamic forces initially result in a left-right shunt, which develops into severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and elevated vascular resistance. Ultimately, due to increased pulmonary vascular resistance, the left-to-right shunt becomes a right-to-left shunt, resulting in significant hypoxemia and cyanosis. PAH is a mean pulmonary arterial pressure greater than 25 mmHg while resting or 30 mmHg when exercising. This may occur in large shunts or complex, unrepaired congenital heart disease as early as the first decade of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mixing of pulmonary and systemic blood and variations in pulmonary blood flow and PVR initiate the pathophysiologic mechanisms of truncus arteriosus. PVR is relatively high starting the 1st week of life. Shunting of mixed oxygenated and deoxygenated blood into the systemic circuit increases, causing mild cyanosis. Pulmonary overcirculation causes congestive heart failure as PVR drops and PBF rises. If untreated, pulmonary overcirculation eventually leads to irreversible pulmonary vascular disease with fixed pulmonary hypertension or Eisenmenger syndrome. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Fetal Circulation -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: However, the shunt can reverse later in life if the left-to-right shunt goes uncorrected. With the left-to-right shunt, there can be a severe overload of the right heart due to increased blood flow, causing an increase in pulmonary vascular resistance, which causes pulmonary hypertension. Eventually, the right ventricle hypertrophies and the pressure on the right side of the heart becomes more significant than on the left side. As a result, the shunt reverses and becomes right-to-left. Deoxygenated blood starts entering systemic circulation, and the baby can present with cyanosis. This switching of flow from left-to-right to right-to-left is known as Eisenmenger syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Central Aortopulmonary Shunt -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Advantages
retrieved_docs.content: Suitable for small children with small peripheral vessels Avoids distortion of the pulmonary branch arteries Provides equal blood flow to both branch pulmonary arteries Lower occlusion rate compared to BTT shunts Avoids subclavian artery steal syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Newborn -- Pathophysiology -- Cardiovascular System
retrieved_docs.content: In the cases of shunts, the severity of the symptoms is determined by the size of the shunt and the amount of blood being shunted. Small left-to-right (acyanotic) shunts are typically benign and do not require surgical intervention. However, larger left-to-right shunts will eventually need surgical intervention to avoid chronic volume overload of the heart and, eventually, pulmonary arterial hypertension. Also, if these shunts are not fixed, a left-to-right shunt can evolve and cause a reversal of blood flow from right to left, leading to Eisenmenger syndrome. [14] Right-to-left (cyanotic) shunts allow the deoxygenated blood from the systemic circulation to bypass the lungs and return to the body without becoming oxygenated. These shunts are more severe, and cyanosis is typically present at birth.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Heart -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The term shunt refers to abnormal communication between vascular structures, and these shunts permit non-physiologic blood flow along pressure gradients. Blood flowing from the right side of the heart to the left side is termed a “right-to-left” shunt, while blood flowing from the left side of the heart to the right side is a “left-to-right” shunt. Right-to-left shunt implies the circulation of deoxygenated blood (i.e., blood that has yet to reach the pulmonary system) to the systemic circulation. Patients with a right to left shunts present with cyanosis and at increased risk for paradoxical embolism and the associated complications like infarction and abscess formation. In contrast, left-to-right shunts do not present with cyanosis since the systemic circulation still receives oxygenated blood. However, the increase in pulmonary blood load from the left-to-right shunt leads to profound pathological responses, partly due to the pulmonary system being a “low-pressure” system incapable of withstanding the increased pressure.The pulmonary arteries typically respond to the increased blood flow and pressure via hypertrophy and vasoconstriction. Eventually, pulmonary vascular resistance approaches systemic levels, creating a shunt reversal (now right-to-left) to distribute deoxygenated blood into the systemic system. The name given this condition of shunt reversal is Eisenmenger syndrome. Once pulmonary hypertension develops, the congenital heart condition progresses beyond irreparability.

retrieved_docs.title: Ventricular Septal Defect -- Pathophysiology -- Gerbode Defects
retrieved_docs.content: In prolonged, large left-to-right shunts, the pulmonary vascular endothelium undergoes irreversible changes, resulting in persistent PAH. When the pressure within the pulmonary circulation exceeds that of the systemic circulation, the shunt direction reverses, leading to a right-to-left shunt. This condition is known as Eisenmenger syndrome and is observed in approximately 10% to 15% of individuals with VSD.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Artery Sling -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Right lung agenesis can also exist with the failure of the development of the right main-stem bronchus. One more associated complexity is associated with VACTERL (vertebral defects, anal, cardiac, tracheal, esophageal, renal, and limb) syndrome. [4] Other genetic syndromes can also be associated, including Down syndrome, Holt-Oram, or Kartagener syndrome. [4] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical follow-up should focus on checking annual CBC, iron studies, kidney function, and uric acid, with a focus on correcting any abnormalities. Patients also should be assessed with pulse oximetry, both with and without supplemental O2. Any abnormalities suggesting hypoxemia warrant further evaluation. Surgical correction of the causative heart defect in adult patients is generally contraindicated [2] . In patients who have developed PAH as a consequence of unrepaired CHD, the defect itself may be acting as a protective measure, preventing worsening of the pulmonary vascular resistance in the face of increasing right ventricular pressure [3] . In children, the development of pulmonary vascular disease (greater than 6 Woods units/m2) with poor vasodilator response is likely to face post-surgical complications, including right heart failure, increased pulmonary hypertension, and hypertensive crises. Classically, it is thought to be of little benefit to undergo such risky procedures with a plethora of potential consequences. Additionally, even with clinical response to vasodilator therapies for the pulmonary arterial hypertension component, there are only a select few case reports that show benefit for the closure of the anatomical defect. However, there is promising evidence that cardiac surgery and close peri- and post-operational management of pulmonary vascular resistance with advanced PAH therapies may improve safety and mitigate downstream problems. [4] [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Central Aortopulmonary Shunt -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Hemodynamic Considerations:
retrieved_docs.content: CAPS function similarly to a patent ductus arteriosus, allowing continuous blood flow during diastole. Careful management is necessary to balance pulmonary and systemic circulation (Qp:Qs ratio), with an ideal ratio of 1:1. Excessive pulmonary blood flow can result in systemic steal, leading to secondary organ dysfunction. Additionally, continuous diastolic flow in the pulmonary arteries can cause coronary steal, increasing the risk of myocardial ischemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortopulmonary Septal Defect -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Echocardiography should completely evaluate the remaining cardiac structures, including evaluating other cardiac diseases such as tetralogy of Fallot, and interruption of the aortic arch, and care should be taken to identify the coronary arteries. Unless the diagnosis is made late or cannot be made with less invasive means, cardiac catheterization adds little to the diagnostic management. When the diagnosis is made after infancy or even into adult life, especially if cyanosis is noted, catheterization could be utilized to evaluate pulmonary vascular resistance as Eisenmenger syndrome can develop. If there is pulmonary vascular disease, reactivity testing should be performed during catheterization to determine the advisability of closure. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2872
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure are all classified as vascular conditions, and the vascular category also encompasses primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ). These entities represent distinct manifestations of abnormal pressure within the circulatory system, illustrating how various forms of hypertension and pulmonary vascular disease fall under the broader umbrella of vascular pathology. Recognizing and managing these vascular pressure abnormalities is essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are both classified as vascular abnormalities, reflecting increased pressure within the circulatory system. The broader vascular category also encompasses severe pressure disorders such as أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate a spectrum of vascular pressure elevations that are important for clinicians to recognize, as they can signal acute emergencies or chronic pulmonary vascular disease and guide appropriate monitoring and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are vascular conditions that represent abnormal increases in the lower and upper values of arterial pressure, respectively. Both of these forms of hypertension are classified as children of the broader Vascular category, which also includes ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) and فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (reactive hypertension). Understanding that these entities are related as subtypes within the vascular system helps clinicians appreciate the full spectrum of blood‑pressure disorders and supports comprehensive assessment and management of patients with vascular hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) are all classified within the broader vascular category. These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1507
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated mean arterial pressure are vascular abnormalities that belong to the broader category of Vascular disorders. Within the Vascular category, additional child conditions include أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Recognizing that these various forms of elevated arterial pressure—whether systemic diastolic, mean arterial, hypertensive crisis, or primary pulmonary hypertension—are interconnected components of vascular pathology is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are classified as vascular conditions. Within the broader vascular category, specific disorders such as ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) are also considered vascular children. These entities illustrate that various forms of abnormal blood pressure—whether diastolic, systolic, pulmonary, or systemic—are interconnected under the vascular system, highlighting the importance of recognizing and managing them as related components of cardiovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure are all classified as Vascular conditions, indicating that each represents a form of increased arterial pressure within the vascular system. The Vascular category also encompasses أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate the spectrum of vascular hypertension, ranging from chronic elevations in diastolic, systolic, and mean arterial pressures to acute severe hypertension and pulmonary‑specific hypertension, and recognizing them as children of the Vascular classification aids clinicians in organizing assessment and treatment strategies for hypertension‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition that reflects an overall increase in arterial pressure. Within the broader vascular category, several specific hypertensive disorders are recognized as subtypes, including أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). Although these entities share the common feature of elevated blood pressure, they differ in their underlying mechanisms, affected vascular territories, and clinical manifestations. Recognizing that elevated mean arterial pressure and its vascular subtypes are interrelated helps clinicians accurately diagnose, monitor, and tailor treatment strategies for patients presenting with various forms of hypertension, thereby improving patient care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure is classified as a vascular condition, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of vascular disorders. Within the vascular category, several specific forms of hypertension are recognized, including ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (diastolic hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). These conditions represent different manifestations of high blood pressure affecting various components of the circulatory system. Recognizing that elevated diastolic blood pressure is part of this vascular hypertension spectrum helps clinicians differentiate among the subtypes, assess associated risks, and tailor management strategies for patients with hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2837
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated mean arterial pressure are both classified as vascular conditions. Within the broader vascular category, there are specific subtypes that are considered its “children,” including primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ) and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي). This hierarchical organization highlights that elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, primary pulmonary hypertension, and reactive hypertension are all interconnected as part of the vascular system, emphasizing their relevance to the assessment and management of vascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is defined by the presence of elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure, each of which is a direct manifestation of this condition. Within the vascular domain, these elevated pressure measurements are considered vascular children, reflecting the systemic impact of primary hypertension on the circulatory system. Primary hypertension is also mapped to several related vascular entities: hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي), and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي). In addition, primary hypertension is associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ) and is clinically linked to abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية). Together, these relationships illustrate how primary hypertension not only elevates key blood pressure parameters but also predisposes patients to acute vascular emergencies, distinct hypertension subtypes, pulmonary vascular disease, and radiographic abnormalities, underscoring the importance of comprehensive vascular assessment and monitoring in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) commonly presents with hemodynamic abnormalities such as elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure, both of which are recognized manifestations of the condition. In addition to these direct cardiovascular signs, primary hypertension is associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), indicating a link between systemic and pulmonary vascular pressure elevations. Patients with primary hypertension may also exhibit abnormal findings on chest radiography (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية), a clinical association that can aid in the diagnostic evaluation and monitoring of the disease. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations and associations is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of individuals with primary hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2753
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated systolic blood pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the same category as several other high‑pressure disorders. Within the vascular domain, أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) represents an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that requires immediate medical intervention. ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) denotes a chronic increase in pressure within the pulmonary arteries that occurs without an identifiable secondary cause. فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) describes a pattern in which only the systolic component of arterial pressure is elevated. Together, Elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, and systolic hypertension are all considered child entities of the broader Vascular classification, highlighting their shared relevance to vascular pathology and the importance of recognizing and managing these distinct forms of high blood pressure in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, a term that describes dilation of the blood vessels within the heart. This equivalence indicates that the ambiguous classification of certain cardiac conditions corresponds to a pathological enlargement of the coronary or myocardial vasculature, which can affect cardiac function and warrants appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2104
retrieved_docs.content: The vascular domain encompasses several distinct forms of elevated blood pressure. Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated mean arterial pressure are recognized as specific vascular abnormalities that reflect increased pressure in the circulatory system. Within the same category, the Arabic‑named conditions ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (diastolic hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) are also classified as vascular children. Together, these entities illustrate the range of hypertensive disorders that fall under the broader vascular classification, highlighting the importance of identifying and managing each type to maintain optimal cardiovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.
